title: Diagrams & Clinical Algorithms
text: Quarto renders Mermaid and Graphviz (DOT) from plain text. Edit the logic, re-render, and the diagram updates — ideal for versioned clinical algorithms.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text: The Jupyter engine runs Python code blocks at render time and embeds the results. This page uses numpy, scipy and matplotlib from the dedicated ~/.venvs/quarto environment.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text:
A worked example: one-compartment pharmacokinetics
We model plasma concentration after an IV bolus, \(C(t) = \dfrac{D}{V_d}\,e^{-k_e t}\), with \(k_e = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

dose, Vd, t_half = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0     # mg, L, hours
ke = np.log(2) / t_half
t  = np.linspace(0, 48, 400)
C  = (dose / Vd) * np.exp(-ke * t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2)
ax.axhline(5, ls="--", color="grey", label="MEC (5 mg/L)")
ax.fill_between(t, C, 5, where=(C > 5), alpha=0.12, color="#B71C1C")
ax.set(xlabel="Time (h)", ylabel="Concentration (mg/L)",
       title="One-compartment IV bolus decay")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(alpha=.3)
plt.show()







Figure 1: Plasma concentration–time curve after a 1000 mg IV bolus.





Figure Figure 1 is cross-referenced automatically.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text:
Computation: AUC by numerical integration

from scipy.integrate import trapezoid
auc = trapezoid(C, t)
above_mec = t[C > 5][-1]
print(f"AUC(0–48h)      = {auc:8.1f} mg·h/L")
print(f"Time above MEC  = {above_mec:8.1f} h")

AUC(0–48h)      =    324.6 mg·h/L
Time above MEC  =     23.9 h

title: Observable Plot Master Showcase
text:
TipThe Grammar of Graphics in the Browser



Observable Plot (Plot) is a declarative JavaScript library for exploratory data visualization. It combines composable marks, data transforms (binning, grouping, stacking, dodging, density), faceted small multiples, and interactive tooltips (Plot.tip()) into a concise grammar of graphics interface that executes natively in Quarto without server reliance.




1. Scatterplots & Beeswarm Dodge (Plot.dot, Plot.dodgeX)
Scatterplots assign numerical attributes to \(X\) and \(Y\) dimensions. When overplotting occurs, Plot.dodgeX() or Plot.dodgeY() arranges points into beeswarm distributions without overlap.


Code
// Simulated patient sample dataset
patients = d3.range(120).map(i => {
  const genotype = ["HbSS", "HbS/β0", "HbS/β+", "HbSC"][i % 4];
  const sex = i % 2 === 0 ? "Female" : "Male";
  const hb = genotype === "HbSS" ? 6.5 + Math.random() * 2.5 :
             genotype === "HbS/β0" ? 7.2 + Math.random() * 2.2 :
             genotype === "HbS/β+" ? 9.0 + Math.random() * 2.5 :
             10.5 + Math.random() * 2.5;
  const hbf = genotype === "HbSS" ? 8 + Math.random() * 14 :
              genotype === "HbS/β0" ? 5 + Math.random() * 10 :
              1 + Math.random() * 5;
  const retic = 120 + (15 - hb) * 25 + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 40;
  return { id: `P-${100+i}`, genotype, sex, hb, hbf, retic };
})









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Hemoglobin (Hb) vs Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF) by Genotype",
  width: 760,
  height: 380,
  grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL) →"},
  y: {label: "↑ Fetal Hemoglobin HbF (%)"},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.dot(patients, {
      x: "hb",
      y: "hbf",
      fill: "genotype",
      r: 4.5,
      fillOpacity: 0.8,
      tip: true
    })
  ]
})









Figure 1: Scatterplot of Hemoglobin vs HbF with interactive tooltips and color encoding.






Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Beeswarm Distribution of Reticulocyte Count across Genotypes",
  width: 760,
  height: 280,
  x: {label: "Genotype"},
  y: {label: "Reticulocyte Count (x10⁹/L)", grid: true},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(patients, Plot.dodgeX("dot", {
      x: "genotype",
      y: "retic",
      fill: "genotype",
      r: 4,
      tip: true
    }))
  ]
})









Figure 2: Beeswarm plot arranged via Plot.dodgeX() transform.







2. Histograms & Automated Binning (Plot.rectY, Plot.binX)
The Plot.binX() transform automatically bins continuous numerical data and counts occurrences:


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Hemoglobin Frequency Histogram",
  width: 760,
  height: 320,
  grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL)"},
  y: {label: "↑ Patient Count"},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(patients, Plot.binX({y: "count"}, {x: "hb", fill: "sex", thresholds: 15})),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})









Figure 3: Binned histogram of patient hemoglobin distribution stacked by sex.







3. 2D Density Contours (Plot.density, Plot.contour)
Density contours visualize bivariate continuous distributions without clutter:


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "2D Density Estimation (Hb vs Reticulocytes)",
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL)"},
  y: {label: "Reticulocytes (x10⁹/L)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.density(patients, {x: "hb", y: "retic", stroke: "genotype", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.dot(patients, {x: "hb", y: "retic", fill: "genotype", r: 2, opacity: 0.5})
  ]
})









Figure 4: Kernel Density Contours of Hemoglobin vs Reticulocytes.







4. Grouped & Stacked Bar Charts (Plot.barY, Plot.groupX)
Categorical summaries aggregated using Plot.groupX():


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Mean Hemoglobin Level by Genotype & Sex",
  width: 760,
  height: 320,
  x: {label: "Genotype"},
  y: {label: "Mean Hb (g/dL)", grid: true},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.barY(patients, Plot.groupX({y: "mean"}, {x: "genotype", y: "hb", fill: "sex", fx: "sex"})),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})









Figure 5: Mean Hemoglobin by Genotype and Sex.







5. Linear Regression & Trend Lines (Plot.linearRegressionY)
Statistical trendlines with automatically calculated confidence bands:


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Linear Regression: Reticulocytes vs Hemoglobin",
  width: 760,
  height: 340,
  grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL)"},
  y: {label: "Reticulocytes (x10⁹/L)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(patients, {x: "hb", y: "retic", fill: "genotype", opacity: 0.6}),
    Plot.linearRegressionY(patients, {x: "hb", y: "retic", stroke: "#b71c1c", strokeWidth: 2.5, ci: 0.95})
  ]
})









Figure 6: Linear regression fit with confidence intervals.







6. Boxplots & Statistical Ranges (Plot.boxY)
Boxplots displaying median, interquartile range (\(IQR\)), whiskers, and outlier points:


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Sickle Cell Genotype Hemoglobin Distribution",
  width: 760,
  height: 320,
  x: {label: "Genotype"},
  y: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL)", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.boxY(patients, {x: "genotype", y: "hb", fill: "genotype", tip: true})
  ]
})









Figure 7: Hemoglobin distribution boxplots across sickle genotypes.







7. Faceted Small Multiples (fx, fy)
Faceting creates sub-plots across multiple dimensions for deep cohort comparison:


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Faceted Sub-plots: Hemoglobin vs HbF by Sex and Genotype",
  width: 760,
  height: 420,
  grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hb (g/dL)"},
  y: {label: "HbF (%)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(patients, {
      x: "hb",
      y: "hbf",
      fill: "genotype",
      fx: "sex",
      fy: "genotype",
      r: 3.5,
      tip: true
    })
  ]
})









Figure 8: Faceted grid comparing Hb vs HbF across Genotypes and Sex.







8. Matrix Heatmaps (Plot.cell)
Heatmaps for multi-variable correlation matrix or grid summaries:


Code
// Binned matrix aggregated for cell mark
matrixData = [
  { group: "HbSS", metric: "Anemia Severity", value: 95 },
  { group: "HbSS", metric: "Vaso-occlusion Rate", value: 85 },
  { group: "HbSS", metric: "HbF Protection", value: 40 },
  { group: "HbS/β0", metric: "Anemia Severity", value: 80 },
  { group: "HbS/β0", metric: "Vaso-occlusion Rate", value: 75 },
  { group: "HbS/β0", metric: "HbF Protection", value: 30 },
  { group: "HbS/β+", metric: "Anemia Severity", value: 50 },
  { group: "HbS/β+", metric: "Vaso-occlusion Rate", value: 40 },
  { group: "HbS/β+", metric: "HbF Protection", value: 20 },
  { group: "HbSC", metric: "Anemia Severity", value: 45 },
  { group: "HbSC", metric: "Vaso-occlusion Rate", value: 60 },
  { group: "HbSC", metric: "HbF Protection", value: 15 }
]









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Clinical Severity Heatmap Matrix",
  width: 720,
  height: 280,
  padding: 0.05,
  x: {label: "Genotype"},
  y: {label: "Clinical Parameter"},
  color: {scheme: "Reds", legend: true, label: "Severity Score (0-100)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.cell(matrixData, {x: "group", y: "metric", fill: "value", tip: true}),
    Plot.text(matrixData, {x: "group", y: "metric", text: d => d.value, fill: d => d.value > 60 ? "white" : "black"})
  ]
})









Figure 9: Matrix heatmap showing clinical severity index.







9. Layered marks & curve interpolation (Plot.areaY, lineY, curve)
Plot has no chart types — you layer marks. A time-series below layers a translucent area, a line, and a ruleY baseline; a second panel shows how the curve option changes interpolation (linear vs. catmull-rom vs. step).


Code
// Synthetic ferritin trajectory over 24 weeks of iron chelation
series = d3.range(0, 25).map(w => ({
  week: w,
  ferritin: 2400 * Math.exp(-0.06 * w) + 120 * Math.sin(w / 2) + (d3.randomNormal(0, 60)())
}))









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Serum ferritin during iron chelation",
  width: 760, height: 320, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Weeks on chelation →"},
  y: {label: "↑ Ferritin (µg/L)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([500], {stroke: "#2e7d32", strokeDasharray: "4"}),
    Plot.areaY(series, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", fill: "#B71C1C", fillOpacity: 0.12}),
    Plot.lineY(series, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.2, tip: true})
  ]
})









Figure 10: Layered area + line + baseline — serum ferritin during chelation.






Code
viewof curveType = Inputs.radio(["linear", "catmull-rom", "step", "basis"], {value: "catmull-rom", label: "Curve interpolation:"})









Code
Plot.plot({
  width: 760, height: 220, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Weeks →"}, y: {label: "Ferritin (µg/L)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(series, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", curve: curveType, stroke: "#7f0000", strokeWidth: 2.4}),
    Plot.dot(series, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", r: 2, fill: "#7f0000"})
  ]
})









10. Stacked areas & streamgraphs (stack, offset)
The areaY/barY marks apply an implicit stack transform. Setting offset: "wiggle" produces a streamgraph — ideal for cohort composition over time.


Code
composition = d3.range(0, 25).flatMap(m =>
  ["HbSS", "HbS/β0", "HbS/β+", "HbSC"].map((g, i) => ({
    month: m, genotype: g,
    n: 20 + 8 * Math.sin(m / 4 + i) + i * 6 + d3.randomNormal(0, 3)()
  })))









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Clinic cohort composition over time (streamgraph)",
  width: 760, height: 300,
  x: {label: "Month →"}, y: {label: null},
  color: {legend: true, scheme: "Reds"},
  marks: [
    Plot.areaY(composition, {x: "month", y: "n", fill: "genotype", offset: "wiggle", curve: "basis", tip: true})
  ]
})









Figure 11: Streamgraph of clinic cohort composition by genotype.






11. Rolling averages & normalisation (Plot.windowY, Plot.normalizeY)
The window transform smooths a noisy signal (moving average); the normalize transform re-indexes series to a common baseline for fair comparison.


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Reticulocyte signal: raw vs 5-week moving average",
  width: 760, height: 300, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Weeks →"}, y: {label: "Reticulocytes"},
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(series, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", stroke: "#ccc", strokeWidth: 1}),
    Plot.lineY(series, Plot.windowY({k: 5, reduce: "mean"}, {x: "week", y: "ferritin", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.5}))
  ]
})









Figure 12: Raw signal with a 5-point moving average overlaid.






12. Parts of a whole (Plot.waffleY)
The waffle mark renders counts as unit squares — a friendlier alternative to pie charts for treatment allocation.


Code
allocation = [
  {arm: "Emicizumab prophylaxis", n: 63},
  {arm: "Factor VIII prophylaxis", n: 41},
  {arm: "On-demand", n: 18}
]









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Trial arm allocation (1 square = 1 patient)",
  width: 760, height: 220,
  color: {legend: true, scheme: "Reds"},
  marks: [Plot.waffleY(allocation, {x: "arm", y: "n", fill: "arm"})]
})









Figure 13: Waffle chart of trial arm allocation.






13. Hexbin density (Plot.hexbin)
The hexbin transform aggregates 2-D points into hexagonal bins — a scalable alternative to overplotted scatterplots.


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Hexbin density (Hb vs reticulocytes)",
  width: 760, height: 360, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin (g/dL) →"}, y: {label: "↑ Reticulocytes"},
  color: {scheme: "Reds", legend: true, label: "count"},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(patients, Plot.hexbin({r: "count", fill: "count"}, {x: "hb", y: "retic"})),
    Plot.frame()
  ]
})









Figure 14: Hexbin density of Hb vs reticulocytes; colour = count.






14. Change & fields (Plot.arrow, Plot.vector)
The arrow mark connects a start and end point (e.g. before/after treatment); the vector mark draws little arrows for magnitude + direction fields.


Code
prePost = ["HbSS", "HbS/β0", "HbS/β+", "HbSC"].map((g, i) => ({
  genotype: g, hbfBefore: [8, 5, 3, 2][i], hbfAfter: [22, 16, 9, 6][i]
}))









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "HbF response to hydroxycarbamide (before → after)",
  width: 760, height: 280, grid: true, marginLeft: 80,
  x: {label: "Fetal haemoglobin HbF (%) →"}, y: {label: null},
  marks: [
    Plot.arrow(prePost, {x1: "hbfBefore", x2: "hbfAfter", y: "genotype", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2, bend: true}),
    Plot.dot(prePost, {x: "hbfBefore", y: "genotype", fill: "#ccc", r: 4}),
    Plot.dot(prePost, {x: "hbfAfter", y: "genotype", fill: "#7f0000", r: 5, tip: true})
  ]
})









Figure 15: Arrow mark: rise in HbF after hydroxycarbamide, by genotype.






15. Ridgeline small multiples (facets + Plot.areaY)
Faceting on fy with a density-like area produces a ridgeline plot — comparing distributions across genotypes.


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Hemoglobin distribution by genotype (ridgeline)",
  width: 760, height: 300, marginLeft: 70,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin (g/dL) →"},
  fy: {label: null},
  color: {scheme: "Reds"},
  marks: [
    Plot.areaY(patients, Plot.binX({y: "count"}, {x: "hb", fy: "genotype", fill: "genotype", curve: "basis", thresholds: 20})),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})









Figure 16: Ridgeline of hemoglobin distribution by genotype.






16. Comparison (Plot.differenceY)
The difference mark fills the gap between two series, colouring gains and shortfalls differently — a natural fit for observed vs target trough levels.


Code
troughs = d3.range(0, 25).map(w => ({
  week: w, observed: 45 + 15 * Math.sin(w / 3) + d3.randomNormal(0, 4)(), target: 50
}))









Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Observed trough vs 50% target",
  width: 760, height: 300, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Weeks →"}, y: {label: "Factor level (%)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.differenceY(troughs, {x: "week", y1: "target", y2: "observed", positiveFill: "#2e7d32", negativeFill: "#B71C1C"}),
    Plot.lineY(troughs, {x: "week", y: "observed", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 1.2})
  ]
})









Figure 17: Difference mark: observed factor trough vs 50% target.






17. Hierarchies (Plot.tree)
The tree mark lays out hierarchical data from a delimited path — here a mini classification of bleeding disorders.


Code
taxonomy = [
  "Bleeding/Coagulation factor/Haemophilia A (FVIII)",
  "Bleeding/Coagulation factor/Haemophilia B (FIX)",
  "Bleeding/Coagulation factor/Rare factor deficiency",
  "Bleeding/Von Willebrand/Type 1",
  "Bleeding/Von Willebrand/Type 2",
  "Bleeding/Von Willebrand/Type 3",
  "Bleeding/Platelet/Glanzmann",
  "Bleeding/Platelet/Bernard-Soulier"
].map(path => ({path}))









Code
Plot.plot({
  width: 760, height: 320, axis: null, margin: 10, marginLeft: 40, marginRight: 200,
  marks: Plot.tree(taxonomy, {path: "path", delimiter: "/", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 1.5})
})









Figure 18: Tree layout of a bleeding-disorder classification.






18. Maps & projections (Plot.geo, Plot.sphere, Plot.graticule)
Plot speaks GeoJSON and D3 projections natively. This self-contained globe uses an orthographic projection with a sphere, graticule, and plotted centres — no external shapefiles required.


Code
centres = [
  {name: "Bangor", lon: -4.13, lat: 53.23},
  {name: "London", lon: -0.13, lat: 51.51},
  {name: "Paris (ISTH)", lon: 2.35, lat: 48.86},
  {name: "New York", lon: -74.0, lat: 40.71}
]









Code
Plot.plot({
  width: 480, height: 480,
  projection: {type: "orthographic", rotate: [10, -40]},
  marks: [
    Plot.sphere({fill: "#f7f3f3", stroke: "#ddd"}),
    Plot.graticule({stroke: "#e2c9c9"}),
    Plot.dot(centres, {x: "lon", y: "lat", fill: "#B71C1C", r: 5}),
    Plot.text(centres, {x: "lon", y: "lat", text: "name", dy: -10, fontWeight: 600, fill: "#7f0000"})
  ]
})









Figure 19: Orthographic globe with graticule and haemophilia-centre markers.






19. Continuous fields (Plot.raster, Plot.contour)
The raster and contour marks visualise a scalar field over a 2-D domain — here an illustrative “risk surface” as a function of two lab values.


Code
Plot.plot({
  title: "Illustrative risk surface f(Hb, platelets)",
  width: 760, height: 340,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin (g/dL) →"}, y: {label: "↑ Platelets (×10⁹/L)"},
  color: {scheme: "Reds", legend: true, label: "risk"},
  marks: [
    Plot.raster({
      x1: 4, x2: 16, y1: 0, y2: 400,
      fill: (x, y) => Math.max(0, (14 - x) / 10) + Math.max(0, (50 - y) / 50)
    }),
    Plot.contour({
      x1: 4, x2: 16, y1: 0, y2: 400,
      fill: (x, y) => Math.max(0, (14 - x) / 10) + Math.max(0, (50 - y) / 50),
      stroke: "white", strokeOpacity: 0.4
    })
  ]
})









Figure 20: Raster + contour of an illustrative risk surface.






20. The auto mark (Plot.auto)
Plot.auto inspects your data and picks a sensible mark and transform — the fastest way to “just show me the data”.


Code
Plot.plot({
  height: 300,
  marks: [Plot.auto(patients, {x: "hb", y: "retic", color: "genotype"})]
})









Figure 21: Plot.auto chooses the encoding automatically.






21. Interaction: crosshair, and a reactive Plot
The crosshair mark reads out the nearest value on the axes without obscuring the data. And because a Plot is just a value, a viewof input can rebuild it live — the pattern behind interactive dashboards.


Code
viewof minHbF = Inputs.range([0, 20], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "Show patients with HbF ≥ (%)"})









Code
Plot.plot({
  width: 760, height: 360, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Hemoglobin (g/dL) →"}, y: {label: "↑ Reticulocytes"},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(patients.filter(d => d.hbf >= minHbF), {x: "hb", y: "retic", stroke: "genotype"}),
    Plot.crosshair(patients.filter(d => d.hbf >= minHbF), {x: "hb", y: "retic"})
  ]
})









Figure 22: Reactive scatter with a live crosshair; filter with the slider.






22. The complete Plot vocabulary
Everything below runs natively in Quarto OJS. This one page exercises the full grammar — marks × transforms × scales × facets × projections × interactions.

MarksTransformsScales & facetsInteraction









Category
Marks




Points
dot · image · text · vector · spike


Lines & areas
line · area · link · arrow · difference · bollinger


Bars & rects
barX / barY · rectX / rectY · cell · waffle · tick


Distribution
boxX / boxY · density · contour · raster · hexgrid


Statistical
linearRegressionX / Y · bollinger


Spatial
geo · sphere · graticule · delaunay · voronoi


Structure
tree · frame · grid · axis · rule


Meta
auto (chooses for you) · tip · crosshair








Category
Transforms




Aggregate
binX / binY / bin · group · hexbin


Layout
stack (offset: wiggle / center) · dodge (beeswarm)


Smoothing
window (rolling mean) · map · normalize


Selection
filter · select · sort · shift · interval


Hierarchy
tree · centroid












Feature
Options




Position
linear · log · pow · sqrt · symlog · time · band · point


Colour
scheme (Reds, YlGnBu…) · sequential · diverging · ordinal · legend


Facets
fx · fy — small multiples across rows/columns


Curves
linear · basis · catmull-rom · step · natural · monotone








Feature
Use




tip: true
details-on-demand tooltip via the pointer transform


Plot.crosshair
axis read-out of the nearest datum


Plot.pointer
filter to the nearest point


viewof + Plot
rebuild the plot reactively from an input












TipOne grammar, every figure



Plot has no chart types — you compose marks over scales, reshape with transforms, split with facets, and add interaction declaratively. Master these primitives and the entire chart universe above is reachable from a single Plot.plot({...}) call, right inside your .qmd.

title: One source, three manuscripts — HTML, PDF & Word
text: The oldest friction in academic writing is format juggling: the journal wants a Word file, your co-author wants a PDF, the pre-print server wants HTML. Quarto produces all three from one source.

title: One source, three manuscripts — HTML, PDF & Word
text:
Declare the formats, render once
---
title: "A reproducible haematology manuscript"
format:
  html:  default
  pdf:   { documentclass: scrartcl }
  docx:  default
---
Then:
quarto render manuscript.qmd            # builds every format
quarto render manuscript.qmd --to docx  # or just one
Cross-references (@fig-, @tbl-), citations from a .bib file, and figure numbering all travel across formats automatically. Send the Word file to a co-author for track-changes; submit the PDF to the journal; post the HTML as a pre-print — never re-formatting by hand.






TipOpen the rendered outputs



HTML · PDF · Word (.docx) — all three built from the one file.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text: Quarto’s R support is the mature knitr engine that powered R Markdown for a decade. Anything you can do in R — ggplot2, survival, gtsummary, meta — you can do inside a document, with the output embedded automatically.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text:
A Kaplan–Meier curve, from code
```{r}
#| label: fig-km
#| fig-cap: "Overall survival by treatment arm."
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(Time, Survival, colour = Arm)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#7f0000", "#2e7d32")) +
  theme_minimal()
```
Change the dataset, re-render, and the curve redraws itself. The same pattern produces meta-analysis forest plots, gtsummary Table 1s, and regression outputs — the staples of a haematology paper.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text:
Freedom from licences
R and its 20,000 packages are open source. For most analyses I run in clinic and research, I am no longer tied to a costly proprietary statistics licence: the computation, the figure and the manuscript now live in one free toolchain.






TipSee it running live



The R engine page renders a survival curve and a forest plot from scratch. The flagship essay also embeds both, side by side with Python, Julia and Observable.

title: A Quarto book — chapters, cross-refs and an index
text: A book is more than a long document: it needs numbered chapters, a table of contents, cross-chapter references and a single bibliography. Quarto’s book project type handles all of it.

title: A Quarto book — chapters, cross-refs and an index
section: The whole book is a list of chapters
text:
The whole book is a list of chapters
project:
  type: book
book:
  title: "A Short Haematology Handbook"
  author: "Dr Abdul Mannan"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - intro.qmd
    - anaemia.qmd
    - references.qmd
format:
  html: default
  pdf:  { documentclass: scrreprt }
quarto render book            # → an HTML book website
quarto render book --to pdf   # → a typeset PDF
Reference a figure in Chapter 3 from Chapter 1 and the number resolves itself. The output is simultaneously a navigable website and a print-ready PDF — the same content, two finished products.






TipRead the demo book



Open the rendered handbook — a complete multi-chapter Quarto book, built from four .qmd files.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text: R, Python and Julia run when the document is built. Observable JS (ojs) runs when the document is read — in the reader’s browser. That means genuine interactivity on a plain static website, with no server to host or maintain.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text:
A reactive cell
```{ojs}
viewof half_life = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, label: "t½ (hours)"})
```
```{ojs}
Plot.plot({ marks: [Plot.lineY(
  d3.range(0, 48, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: 20*Math.exp(-Math.log(2)/half_life*t)})),
  {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C"})] })
```
Drag the slider and every cell that depends on it recomputes instantly — the reactive model made famous by Observable, built into Quarto and powered by d3 and Observable Plot.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text:
Why teachers love it
A trainee can feel how halving a drug’s half-life changes its concentration curve, or how a diagnostic threshold shifts sensitivity and specificity — by moving a control, not by reading a caption.






TipTry it live



The Observable page and the flagship essay both carry a working half-life slider you can drag right now.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text: A good teaching page reveals detail on demand. Quarto ships the building blocks as simple Markdown fences — no JavaScript to write.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text:
Tabsets: one concept, several views
::: {.panel-tabset}
## Film        …blood-film description…
## Immunophenotype  …markers…
## Management   …first-line therapy…
:::
Readers click between Film, Immunophenotype and Management without leaving the page — ideal for comparing differentials.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text:
Callouts carry the signal
::: {.callout-warning}
## Red flag
Blasts + coagulopathy → treat as APL until proven otherwise.
:::
Five callout styles (note, tip, important, warning, caution) give teaching material a consistent visual grammar, and collapsible callouts hide a model answer until the learner is ready.






TipSee it live



The interactive teaching demo combines tabsets, callouts and a live Observable widget in one lesson page.

title: Diagrams as code — Mermaid & Graphviz
text: Drawing a flowchart by hand in PowerPoint is slow, and the moment guidance changes you redraw it. Quarto lets you write diagrams as text and compiles them to scalable vector graphics.

title: Diagrams as code — Mermaid & Graphviz
section: A decision algorithm in plain text
text:
A decision algorithm in plain text
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Chromatogram"] --> B{"HbS present?"}
  B -->|Yes| C["Evaluate % HbS"]
  B -->|No|  D["Evaluate % HbA2"]
  C -->|38–45%| E["Sickle cell trait"]
  D -->|3.5–7%| G["β-thalassaemia trait"]
```
That text renders as a labelled, colour-coded flowchart. Because it is text, it diffs cleanly in git — you can see exactly how a pathway changed between versions, and reviewers can comment on a line.
Mermaid covers flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts and mind-maps; Graphviz (dot) handles dense graphs and inheritance trees. Both are built in.

The Diagrams & algorithms page compiles several clinical pathways, including the HbS / HbA2 interpretation tree.

title: About this blog
text:
I am Dr Abdul Mannan, a Consultant Haematologist and Director of the Bangor Haemophilia Centre. This blog is two things at once: a personal record of learning to publish with code, and a living demonstration of what Quarto can build.
Every post here is a genuine, rendered example — not a screenshot. The survival curves are computed in R, the pharmacokinetic models in Python, the interactive sliders in your own browser, and the manuscripts, slides, book, EPUB and dashboard are all built from the same plain-text sources you can read in each post.

title: About this blog
text:
Why I built it
For most of my career, turning an idea into a finished scientific product meant depending on a publisher, a statistician or a designer — and on costly proprietary software. Quarto, with R and Python, removed many of those barriers. The analysis, the narrative, the references, the figures and the final publication now live in one reproducible workflow that I control.
Tools do not replace sound statistical judgement, scientific review or good design. But they remove the friction between an idea and its final form.







Do not be embarrassed by your first attempt. Every polished skill begins with an imperfect beginning. Keep learning. Keep building. Keep creating.










ImportantCitation integrity



Bibliographic references in these demonstration posts illustrate the mechanism. Before any real publication, every citation is re-verified live against PubMed and Crossref under my Citation Integrity Protocol.

title: Knowledge has no limits.
text:
A reproducible publishing lab




From a badly aligned PowerPoint slide on a hospital laboratory computer in the early 2000s — to coding my own books, reports, websites, dashboards and scientific figures. This blog is that journey, and a live demonstration of everything Quarto can build from a single source file.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
1. R Engine ({r}): Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve
Using R and ggplot2, we generate a publication-ready Kaplan-Meier survival curve for clinical trials:


Code
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
time <- seq(0, 36, by=1)
surv_control <- exp(-0.06 * time) + rnorm(length(time), 0, 0.01)
surv_treated <- exp(-0.025 * time) + rnorm(length(time), 0, 0.01)

df <- data.frame(
  Time = rep(time, 2),
  Survival = c(pmax(0, surv_control), pmax(0, surv_treated)),
  Arm = factor(rep(c("Standard Care", "Targeted Therapy"), each=length(time)))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x=Time, y=Survival, color=Arm, linetype=Arm)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#7f0000", "#2e7d32")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent, limits=c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title="Overall Survival in Relapsed/Refractory Disease",
       subtitle="Kaplan-Meier Survival Estimates by Treatment Arm",
       x="Follow-up Duration (Months)", y="Overall Survival (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom", plot.title=element_text(face="bold", color="#7f0000"))








Figure 1: Kaplan-Meier overall survival curve generated dynamically with R and ggplot2.





As demonstrated in Figure 1, targeted therapy significantly extends overall survival compared to standard care.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
2. R Engine ({r}): Meta-Analysis Forest Plot
Forest plots synthesize relative risk or hazard ratios across clinical studies:


Code
trials <- data.frame(
  Study = c("UK Haem Study (2022)", "EHA Registry (2024)", "ASH Consortium (2025)", "Blood Doctor Cohort (2026)", "Pooled Meta-Analysis"),
  HR = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.61, 0.58, 0.65),
  Lower = c(0.51, 0.58, 0.45, 0.42, 0.54),
  Upper = c(0.91, 0.89, 0.82, 0.80, 0.78),
  Summary = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

ggplot(trials, aes(x=HR, y=reorder(Study, c(5,4,3,2,1)))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=1, linetype="dashed", color="grey40") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=Lower, xmax=Upper), height=0.25, color="#7f0000", linewidth=1) +
  geom_point(aes(shape=Summary, size=Summary, fill=Summary), color="#7f0000") +
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(21, 23)) +
  scale_size_manual(values=c(3.5, 5)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("#7f0000", "#ffd5d5")) +
  labs(title="Forest Plot of Treatment Hazard Ratios",
       x="Hazard Ratio (95% CI)", y=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
  theme(legend.position="none", axis.text.y=element_text(face="bold"))








Figure 2: Clinical Trial Forest Plot showing Hazard Ratios (HR) and 95% Confidence Intervals.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
section: 3. Python engine ({python}) — pharmacokinetics & a pandas table
text:
3. Python engine ({python}) — pharmacokinetics & a pandas table
The same workflow speaks Python. This is the source that models one-compartment IV-bolus kinetics and builds a pandas summary table:
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, pandas as pd

dose, Vd, t_half = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0
ke = np.log(2) / t_half
t  = np.linspace(0, 48, 200)
C  = (dose / Vd) * np.exp(-ke * t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2, label="Concentration C(t)")
ax.axhline(5, ls="--", color="grey", label="MEC (5 mg/L)")
ax.fill_between(t, C, 5, where=(C > 5), alpha=0.15, color="#B71C1C")
ax.set(xlabel="Time (h)", ylabel="Plasma concentration (mg/L)")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(alpha=0.3); plt.show()






TipWhy the Python figure isn’t drawn here



A Quarto document runs one execution engine at a time. This page is driven by R’s knitr — which is why the survival curve and forest plot above are live. The identical Python model runs live on its own page: watch it compute the curve, the numerical AUC and the table on the Python engine page, and drive the clinical dashboard.

5. Observable JS Engine ({ojs}): Client-Side Interactivity
Observable JS (ojs) runs natively in the reader’s browser with zero backend server requirement:


Code
viewof half_life = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, step: 1, label: "Adjust Half-life t½ (hours):"})









Code
ke_ojs = Math.log(2) / half_life
data_ojs = d3.range(0, 48.5, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: (1000 / 50) * Math.exp(-ke_ojs * t)}))

Plot.plot({
  width: 700, height: 320,
  x: {label: "Time (hours) →"},
  y: {label: "↑ Concentration (mg/L)", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([5], {stroke: "grey", strokeDasharray: "4"}),
    Plot.areaY(data_ojs, {x: "t", y: "C", fill: "#B71C1C", fillOpacity: 0.15}),
    Plot.lineY(data_ojs, {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.2})
  ]
})

























Code
md`Dynamic Calculation: At **t½ = ${half_life} hours**, the elimination rate constant is **kₑ = ${ke_ojs.toFixed(4)} h⁻¹**.`

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
7. Mathematical Equations & Citation Integrity
Mathematical expressions render using LaTeX / MathJax formatting:
\[\text{Hazard Ratio (HR)} = \frac{h_1(t)}{h_0(t)} = \exp\left( \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots \right)\]
\[\text{AUC}_{0 \to \infty} = \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t) \, dt = \frac{C_0}{k_e} = \frac{Dose}{CL}\]
Citations are linked to verified PubMed entries per the Citation Integrity Protocol (Donze et al. 1997; Mrabet et al. 1985).

title: The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation
text: Some problems — large matrix differential equations, Monte-Carlo simulations, population PK models — are simply slow in interpreted languages. Julia compiles to native code and runs close to C speed, and Quarto speaks it natively.

title: The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation
text:
Same document, a third language
```{julia}
using Plots
gr()
t = 0:0.5:48
C = 20.0 .* exp.(-(log(2)/12) .* t)
plot(t, C, lw = 2.4, color = :firebrick, xlabel = "Time (h)")
```
The remarkable part is not Julia alone — it is that an R chunk, a Python chunk and a Julia chunk can sit in one source file, each doing what it is best at, and render into one seamless document.






TipSee it running live



The Julia engine page executes a Plots.jl computation at render time. The project’s Julia environment is pinned via Project.toml / Manifest.toml so the result is fully reproducible.








Note



Julia’s first render compiles its packages and can take a minute; Quarto’s freeze then caches the result so subsequent builds are instant.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text: When a document is rendered, Quarto hands every ```{python} block to a Jupyter kernel, executes it, and embeds the result — a figure, a table, a value — back into the page. Nothing is copied and pasted, so nothing can go stale.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text:
The idea in three lines
A Python code cell is written with a {python} engine tag and optional YAML-style options after #|:
```{python}
#| label: fig-pk
#| fig-cap: "Plasma concentration after a 1000 mg IV bolus."
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
t = np.linspace(0, 48, 400)
C = (1000/50) * np.exp(-np.log(2)/12 * t)
plt.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2); plt.show()
```
#| label: fig-pk makes the figure cross-referenceable — write @fig-pk anywhere and Quarto inserts the correct number and a hyperlink.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text:
Why it matters clinically
Every pharmacokinetic curve, every summary table, every p-value is regenerated from the data each time the paper is built. Re-run with new numbers and the prose, figures and tables all move together. That is reproducibility you can defend in front of a reviewer.






TipSee it running live



The Python engine page executes this pharmacokinetic model in full — curve, numerical AUC, and a pandas table — all at render time.


The environment is pinned outside the synced Drive folder (~/.venvs/quarto) so package installs never trigger a cloud-sync storm.

title: Julia Engine
text: The Julia engine runs Julia via IJulia. Great when an algorithm needs raw numerical speed. Plots use Plots.jl (GR backend). We set GKSwstype=100 so GR renders straight to a file on a headless machine.

title: Julia Engine
section: A worked example: IV bolus decay
text:
A worked example: IV bolus decay

using Plots
ENV["GKSwstype"] = "100"   # headless GR: write to file, no display server
gr()

dose, Vd, thalf = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0
ke = log(2) / thalf
t  = 0:0.5:48
C  = (dose / Vd) .* exp.(-ke .* t)

plot(t, C, lw = 2.4, color = :firebrick, label = "C(t)",
     xlabel = "Time (h)", ylabel = "Concentration (mg/L)",
     title = "Julia — one-compartment IV bolus", legend = :topright)
hline!([5], ls = :dash, color = :grey, label = "MEC")






Figure 1: Julia + Plots.jl: concentration–time curve.

title: Julia Engine
text:
An algorithm: Newton–Raphson root finding
Solve for the time at which concentration reaches the 5 mg/L threshold. (The cell is self-contained.)

dose, Vd, ke = 1000.0, 50.0, log(2) / 12   # constants

f(x)  = (dose / Vd) * exp(-ke * x) - 5.0
df(x) = -ke * (dose / Vd) * exp(-ke * x)

x = 10.0
for _ in 1:20
    x -= f(x) / df(x)
end
println("Threshold crossed at t = ", round(x, digits = 2), " h")
println("Analytic solution      = ", round(log((dose / Vd) / 5) / ke, digits = 2), " h")

Threshold crossed at t = 24.0 h
Analytic solution      = 24.0 h

title: Observable JS Engine & Interactive Studio
text:
TipThe Power of Observable JS (OJS) in Quarto



Observable JS cells run entirely in the reader’s web browser using a topological dataflow graph. When an input widget changes, only cells that depend on that variable re-evaluate automatically. Plot, D3, htl, and Inputs are natively built-in—no installation or server configuration required.




1. Interactive Pharmacokinetic & Clinical Dashboard
Adjust the parameters below to explore live pharmacokinetic clearance curves and patient cohort simulations in real time:


Code
viewof dose = Inputs.range([250, 2000], {value: 1000, step: 25, label: "IV Bolus Dose (mg):"})
viewof thalf = Inputs.range([4, 48], {value: 12, step: 1, label: "Half-life t½ (hours):"})
viewof Vd = Inputs.range([20, 100], {value: 50, step: 2, label: "Vol. Distribution Vd (L):"})
viewof mec = Inputs.range([1, 25], {value: 5, step: 0.5, label: "Target MEC (mg/L):"})
viewof showThreshold = Inputs.toggle({label: "Show Threshold Line", value: true})







































Code
ke = Math.log(2) / thalf
C0 = dose / Vd
auc = dose / (Vd * ke)
timeToMec = C0 > mec ? (Math.log(C0 / mec) / ke) : 0

// Time grid series
pkData = d3.range(0, 72.5, 0.5).map(t => {
  const conc = C0 * Math.exp(-ke * t);
  return {
    t: t,
    C: conc,
    above: conc >= mec ? "Above Target" : "Below Target"
  };
})







































Code
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 380,
  marginRight: 40,
  x: {label: "Time Post-Dose (hours) →", grid: true, domain: [0, 72]},
  y: {label: "↑ Plasma Concentration (mg/L)", grid: true, domain: [0, Math.max(C0 * 1.05, mec * 1.2)]},
  color: {
    domain: ["Above Target", "Below Target"],
    range: ["#2e7d32", "#B71C1C"],
    legend: true
  },
  marks: [
    showThreshold ? Plot.ruleY([mec], {stroke: "#d68b00", strokeDasharray: "4,4", strokeWidth: 1.8}) : null,
    showThreshold ? Plot.text([{t: 70, C: mec}], {x: "t", y: "C", text: d => `MEC: ${mec} mg/L`, dy: -10, fill: "#d68b00", textAnchor: "end"}) : null,
    Plot.areaY(pkData, {x: "t", y: "C", fill: "#B71C1C", fillOpacity: 0.12}),
    Plot.lineY(pkData, {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.5}),
    Plot.dot(pkData.filter((d, i) => i % 8 === 0), {x: "t", y: "C", fill: "#7f0000", r: 3, tip: true})
  ]
})









Figure 1: Live-updating pharmacokinetic concentration-time curve with interactive tooltips.






Code
md`
### Live PK Parameters Summary
- **Peak Initial Concentration ($C_0$):** **${C0.toFixed(1)} mg/L**
- **Elimination Rate Constant ($k_e$):** **${ke.toFixed(4)} h⁻¹**
- **Total AUC ($0 \to \infty$):** **${auc.toFixed(1)} mg·h/L**
- **Time Above Target MEC (${mec} mg/L):** **${timeToMec.toFixed(1)} hours**
`










2. Interactive Patient Cohort Table & Filtering
Observable Inputs include Inputs.table() and Inputs.search(), offering spreadsheet-style sorting and filtering right inside the browser:


Code
// Simulated patient cohort dataset
cohortData = [
  { id: "PT-101", age: 45, sex: "Female", genotype: "HbSS", hb: 7.2, hbf: 14.5, hydroxyurea: "Yes", response: "Complete" },
  { id: "PT-102", age: 52, sex: "Male", genotype: "HbS/β0", hb: 8.1, hbf: 8.2, hydroxyurea: "Yes", response: "Partial" },
  { id: "PT-103", age: 29, sex: "Female", genotype: "HbAS", hb: 12.8, hbf: 1.1, hydroxyurea: "No", response: "Carrier" },
  { id: "PT-104", age: 61, sex: "Male", genotype: "HbSS", hb: 6.9, hbf: 18.2, hydroxyurea: "Yes", response: "Complete" },
  { id: "PT-105", age: 38, sex: "Female", genotype: "HbS/β+", hb: 9.8, hbf: 6.4, hydroxyurea: "No", response: "Partial" },
  { id: "PT-106", age: 44, sex: "Male", genotype: "HbSC", hb: 10.4, hbf: 3.5, hydroxyurea: "No", response: "Moderate" },
  { id: "PT-107", age: 57, sex: "Female", genotype: "HbSS", hb: 7.5, hbf: 12.1, hydroxyurea: "Yes", response: "Complete" },
  { id: "PT-108", age: 33, sex: "Male", genotype: "HbS/β0", hb: 7.8, hbf: 9.0, hydroxyurea: "Yes", response: "Partial" }
]









Code
viewof selectedGenotype = Inputs.checkbox(
  ["HbSS", "HbS/β0", "HbS/β+", "HbSC", "HbAS"],
  { value: ["HbSS", "HbS/β0", "HbS/β+", "HbSC"], label: "Filter Genotype:" }
)









Code
filteredCohort = cohortData.filter(d => selectedGenotype.includes(d.genotype))









Code
Inputs.table(filteredCohort, {
  columns: ["id", "age", "sex", "genotype", "hb", "hbf", "hydroxyurea", "response"],
  header: {
    id: "Patient ID",
    age: "Age (yrs)",
    sex: "Sex",
    genotype: "Genotype",
    hb: "Hb (g/dL)",
    hbf: "HbF (%)",
    hydroxyurea: "Hydroxyurea",
    response: "Clinical Response"
  },
  layout: "auto"
})









Code
// Summary statistics chart based on filtered selection
Plot.plot({
  title: "Hemoglobin Distribution by Genotype in Selected Cohort",
  width: 720,
  height: 280,
  x: {label: "Genotype"},
  y: {label: "Hemoglobin Hb (g/dL)", domain: [0, 15]},
  marks: [
    Plot.barY(filteredCohort, {x: "genotype", y: "hb", fill: "#7f0000", opacity: 0.7, tip: true}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})










3. Complete Observable Inputs Reference
Observable HQ provides a full suite of reactive input primitives for data apps:








Input Function
Purpose
Syntax Example




Inputs.range([min, max], opts)
Numeric slider
viewof x = Inputs.range([0, 100], {step: 1})


Inputs.select(values, opts)
Single / Multi dropdown
viewof drug = Inputs.select(["Drug A", "Drug B"])


Inputs.radio(values, opts)
Radio button group
viewof arm = Inputs.radio(["Control", "Active"])


Inputs.checkbox(values, opts)
Multi-checkbox selection
viewof sites = Inputs.checkbox(["Site 1", "Site 2"])


Inputs.toggle(opts)
Boolean switch
viewof logScale = Inputs.toggle({label: "Log Scale"})


Inputs.text(opts)
Text search input
viewof query = Inputs.text({placeholder: "Search..."})


Inputs.color(opts)
Color picker
viewof color = Inputs.color({value: "#7f0000"})


Inputs.table(data, opts)
Interactive data table
Inputs.table(data, {columns: [...]})


Inputs.search(data, opts)
Data search engine
viewof search = Inputs.search(data)






4. The reactive model — order does not matter
In vanilla JavaScript, code runs top to bottom. In Observable it runs in topological order: a cell runs after the cells it references, regardless of source order — like a spreadsheet. Below, the summary cell is written before the cells that define its inputs, yet it resolves correctly and re-runs the instant the slider moves.


Code
viewof platelets = Inputs.range([0, 450], {value: 250, step: 5, label: "Platelet count (×10⁹/L):"})









This cell is written FIRST but depends on cells defined below
md`**Interpretation:** a count of **${platelets}×10⁹/L** is
**${bleedRisk}** — ${bleedRisk === "critical" ? "consider prophylactic transfusion" : bleedRisk === "low" ? "spontaneous bleeding likely" : "no specific action on count alone"}.`









…its dependency, defined AFTERWARDS
bleedRisk = platelets < 10 ? "critical" : platelets < 20 ? "low" : platelets < 50 ? "moderate" : "adequate"













Each OJS cell is a named declaration, not an assignment. Quarto builds the dependency graph and re-runs only affected cells. Names must be unique across the document, and circular references error — exactly the spreadsheet rules.





5. Reactive width
width is a reactive variable from the standard library holding the current column width. Reference it and figures re-lay-out automatically on resize or column change — drag your browser edge and watch this bar re-fit.


Code
Plot.plot({
  width,                                     // ← reactive: no fixed pixel value
  height: 90,
  x: {label: "Half-life t½ (h) →"},
  marks: [Plot.barX([{p:"SHL FVIII", h:12}, {p:"EHL FVIII", h:19}, {p:"BHL FVIII", h:42}],
    {x: "h", y: "p", fill: "#B71C1C", sort: {y: "x"}}), Plot.ruleX([0])]
})









6. mutable — opt-in shared state
Named cells can’t be reassigned. When you genuinely need shared, writable state (a running log, a tally), Observable’s mutable operator is the escape hatch: one cell declares it, other cells write to it, and every reader updates.


Code
mutable transfusionLog = []









Code
viewof addUnit = Inputs.button("🩸 Transfuse 1 unit (+30 ×10⁹/L expected)", {reduce: () => { mutable transfusionLog = [...transfusionLog, {unit: transfusionLog.length + 1, expectedRise: 30, runningTotal: platelets + 30 * (transfusionLog.length + 1)}]; }})









Code
transfusionLog.length
  ? Inputs.table(transfusionLog, {header: {unit: "Unit #", expectedRise: "Δ ×10⁹/L", runningTotal: "Projected count"}})
  : md`*No units logged yet — set the platelet slider in §4, then click the button.*`









7. Generators, now and animation
If a cell yields repeatedly, referencing cells see the latest value, at most once every animation frame. With the reactive now clock and Promises.tick, this drives live simulation — no timers to manage. Here a continuous-infusion model climbs to steady state in real time.


Code
viewof infusionRate = Inputs.range([0.5, 5], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "Infusion rate (× clearance):"})









A generator cell that yields ≤ once every 50 ms
steadyLevel = {
  let c = 0;
  while (true) {
    c = c + (infusionRate * 10 - c) * 0.04;   // first-order approach to steady state
    yield Promises.tick(50, c);
  }
}









Code
html`<div style="font:600 1rem/1.5 Inter, sans-serif">
  Current level: <span style="color:#B71C1C">${steadyLevel.toFixed(1)} mg/L</span>
  <progress max="50" value="${steadyLevel}" style="width:55%;height:14px"></progress>
</div>`









8. Loading data with FileAttachment
FileAttachment("file.csv").csv({typed: true}) loads a data file lazily and type-infers columns. Piped into Inputs.search (a live query box) and Inputs.table, it becomes an instant data explorer — the pattern behind most Observable dashboards. This reads a real CSV that sits beside this .qmd.


Code
factorProducts = FileAttachment("factor-products.csv").csv({typed: true})









Code
viewof productQuery = Inputs.search(factorProducts, {placeholder: "Search products — try EHL, FIX, emicizumab…"})









Code
Inputs.table(productQuery, {
  columns: ["product", "factor", "technology", "half_life_h", "dosing_per_week"],
  header: {product: "Product", factor: "Factor", technology: "Technology", half_life_h: "t½ (h)", dosing_per_week: "Doses/wk"},
  sort: "half_life_h", reverse: true
})









Code
Plot.plot({
  width, height: 300, marginLeft: 100,
  x: {label: "Half-life t½ (hours) →", grid: true},
  y: {label: null},
  color: {legend: true, scheme: "reds"},
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(productQuery, {x: "half_life_h", y: "product", fill: "factor", sort: {y: "x", reverse: true}, tip: true}),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ]
})









9. The cross-language bridge — ojs_define()
The Quarto superpower that the Observable notebook platform can’t match: compute in R, Python or Julia at render time, then hand the result to the browser with ojs_define(). Here R builds pharmacokinetic decay curves; OJS plots them client-side after transpose().


Code
prods <- data.frame(
  product = c("Advate · t½ 12 h", "Elocta · t½ 19 h", "Altuviiio · t½ 42 h"),
  thalf   = c(12, 19, 42.5)
)
grid  <- seq(0, 168, by = 2)
decay <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(nrow(prods)), function(i) {
  data.frame(product = prods$product[i], t = grid,
             level = 100 * exp(-log(2) / prods$thalf[i] * grid))
}))
ojs_define(decayData = decay)




Code
decayCurves = transpose(decayData)     // Quarto helper: R data.frame → array of objects
Plot.plot({
  width, height: 320,
  x: {label: "Time since dose (h) →"},
  y: {label: "↑ % of peak", grid: true, domain: [0, 100]},
  color: {legend: true, scheme: "reds"},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], {stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "3"}),
    Plot.lineY(decayCurves, {x: "t", y: "level", stroke: "product", strokeWidth: 2.4}),
    Plot.tip(decayCurves, Plot.pointer({x: "t", y: "level", stroke: "product"}))
  ]
})
















The three curves make the extended-half-life case visually: a single weekly dose of a BHL molecule holds a trough that standard products lose within days.


10. Maths and Markdown, interpolated
tex renders LaTeX (KaTeX) and md renders Markdown; both accept live interpolation, so equations and prose stay in sync with the inputs above.


Code
tex.block`C(t) \;=\; \frac{D}{V_d}\, e^{-k_e t}, \qquad k_e = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = ${(Math.log(2)/thalf).toFixed(4)}\ \text{h}^{-1}`









11. Importing from Observable notebooks
Because OJS is the Observable runtime, Quarto can import any named cell from a public notebook — code, charts or inputs — with live bindings. Here we import Mike Bostock’s Scrubber and use it as a play/scrub control over the PK timeline.


Code
import {Scrubber} from "@mbostock/scrubber"









Code
viewof scrubT = Scrubber(d3.range(0, 73), {autoplay: false, loop: true, format: h => `t = ${h} h`, delay: 120})









Code
md`At the scrubbed time **t = ${scrubT} h**, the §1 model predicts a plasma level of
**${(C0 * Math.exp(-ke * scrubT)).toFixed(1)} mg/L**.`







The import {x as y} from … and import {chart} with {myData as data} from … variations rename cells or inject your own data into someone else’s chart — treating any notebook as an extensible template.






TipThe Observable standard library, at a glance



Available in any {ojs} cell with no import: FileAttachment · Inputs · Plot · d3 · width · now · mutable · viewof · Generators · Promises.tick · invalidation · html / svg / md / tex / dot (Graphviz). Recommended libraries — _ (Lodash), aq (Arquero), L (Leaflet), mermaid, topojson, vl (Vega-Lite) — load on first use.






 Back to top

title: R Engine — knitr
text: The knitr engine executes R chunks. Packages are loaded from your personal library (~/Library/R/arm64/4.5/library). Here we use ggplot2.

title: R Engine — knitr
text:
Simulated platelet recovery after chemotherapy

library(ggplot2)
set.seed(42)

days      <- 0:28
platelets <- 20 + 380 / (1 + exp(-0.30 * (days - 14)))   # logistic recovery
obs       <- platelets + rnorm(length(days), 0, 9)
df        <- data.frame(day = days, platelets = obs)

ggplot(df, aes(day, platelets)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_line(colour = "#B71C1C", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(colour = "#7f0000", size = 1.8) +
  labs(title = "Simulated platelet recovery",
       x = "Day post-chemotherapy",
       y = expression(Platelets~(10^9/L))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)







Figure 1: Modelled platelet recovery; dashed line marks the 100 ×10⁹/L threshold.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text:
Layered content with tabsets

ConceptMechanismMonitoring


Emicizumab is a bispecific antibody bridging activated factor IX and factor X, mimicking the cofactor function of factor VIII.


It does not require activation and is not affected by FVIII inhibitors — hence its role in haemophilia A with inhibitors.


Standard APTT-based assays are unreliable on emicizumab. Use a bovine-based chromogenic FVIII assay to monitor.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text:
Callouts for emphasis






WarningAssay pitfall



Emicizumab shortens the APTT dramatically and invalidates one-stage FVIII assays.








NoteExpand: dosing summary





Loading 3 mg/kg weekly ×4, then a maintenance schedule (weekly / fortnightly / four-weekly). Verify against the current SmPC before clinical use.
